coredata.dc:title: Thermo-optical characterization of fluorescent materials based on edible vegetable oils

coredata.dc:description: This work evaluated the thermo-optical characterization of several vegetable oils such as Persea gratissima (avocado oil, AO), Mauritia flexuosa Linnaeus filius (buriti oil, BO), Passiflora edulis (passion fruit oil, PFO), and Plukenetia volubilis L. (Sacha inchi oil, SIO). Vegetable oils are nontoxic, nonvolatile, nondepletable, and biodegradable resources used for several applications, such as in the foods, cosmetic, lubricant, coating, and pharmaceutical industries, due to their possible antioxidant and antimicrobial activities. In this work, the refractive index temperature coefficients (dn/dT) of AO, BO, PFO, and SIO were characterized as a function of temperature (296-329 K−1) via a Mach–Zehnder interferometer. The thermal diffusivity (D) and fluorescence quantum efficiency (η) were determined via the dual-beam mode-mismatched thermal lens (TL) technique. Fluorescence and time-resolved fluorescence measurements corroborate the TL results. AO presented a relatively higher η value than other vegetable oils did (BO, PFO, and SIO), and avocado oil was heat treated at 180 °C for the synthesis of carbon dots (CAO-dots). The η and fluorescence lifetime (τ) results obtained for the avocado-based nanoparticles were compared with those of other C-dots using oil as a carbon source, aiming at potential bioapplications.

head.ce:title: Thermo-optical characterization of fluorescent materials based on edible vegetable oils

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work evaluated the thermo-optical characterization of several vegetable oils such as Persea gratissima (avocado oil, AO), Mauritia flexuosa Linnaeus filius (buriti oil, BO), Passiflora edulis (passion fruit oil, PFO), and Plukenetia volubilis L. (Sacha inchi oil, SIO). Vegetable oils are nontoxic, nonvolatile, nondepletable, and biodegradable resources used for several applications, such as in the foods, cosmetic, lubricant, coating, and pharmaceutical industries, due to their possible antioxidant and antimicrobial activities. In this work, the refractive index temperature coefficients (dn/dT) of AO, BO, PFO, and SIO were characterized as a function of temperature (296-329 K−1) via a Mach–Zehnder interferometer. The thermal diffusivity (D) and fluorescence quantum efficiency (η) were determined via the dual-beam mode-mismatched thermal lens (TL) technique. Fluorescence and time-resolved fluorescence measurements corroborate the TL results. AO presented a relatively higher η value than other vegetable oils did (BO, PFO, and SIO), and avocado oil was heat treated at 180 °C for the synthesis of carbon dots (CAO-dots). The η and fluorescence lifetime (τ) results obtained for the avocado-based nanoparticles were compared with those of other C-dots using oil as a carbon source, aiming at potential bioapplications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Natural vegetable oils (Voil) are extracted from readily available and inexpensive bioderived raw materials, but their chemical, physical, and antimicrobial properties have rarely been investigated [1–4]. Voil is derived from several sources and is primarily extracted from seeds. However, various parts of vegetables can be used for oil extraction, such as the pulp of fruits, seeds, leaves, roots, flowers, and stems [1,5,6]. Voil is a nontoxic, nonvolatile, nondepletable, liquid at room temperature, and is a biodegradable resource used for several applications, such as in the food, cosmetic, perfumery, lubricant, coating, and pharmaceutical industries, due to its possible antioxidant and antimicrobial activities [1,5,7,8].

Several vegetable oils, such as avocado, buriti, passion fruit, and Sacha inchi, are edible. However, these oils have wide potential for technological applications, depending on the extraction conditions and the refining process used to remove undesirable components such as free fatty acids and their organoleptic properties while maintaining desirable bioactive components [9–12]. Avocado oil is a potential source of functional fruit oil with several health benefits due to its pharmacological and antimicrobial properties [9–11], and there is technological interest in products based on avocado oil, such as natural emulsifiers, structured lipids, and biodegradable polymers [11]. The oil extracted from Persea gratissima (avocado fruit) pulp can be used without a refining process [10], and this oil is rich in monounsaturated fatty acids and presents nutritional properties similar to those of olive oil [11,12]. Persea Americana Mill is an avocado tree, an oil-rich fruit whose pulp contains different oil yields that depend on the avocado variety [12,13]. On the other hand, buriti oil is extracted from the fruit of Mauritia flexuosa Linnaeus filius, a South American palm tree that often inhabits areas along rivers, savannas, and forests [14–17]. The buriti fruit has a rigid, red, and scaly skin, which covers a soft pulp, with changes in color from dark yellow to reddish due to carotenes [14–17], and its oil is a natural antioxidant and is proposed for potential biodegradable films for food packing to increase shelf-life [18].

The Passifloraceae family produces edible passion fruit and Passiflora edulis. Brazil is the largest edulis species producer, also known as passion fruit, and is a source of biologically active compounds with organoleptic qualities [19–21]. Passion fruit oil has high levels of unsaturated fatty acids, mainly linoleic and oleic acids, and several bioactive compounds, such as carotenoids, tocopherols, and phenolic compounds [22,23]. Therefore, this oil is a potential raw material for the food, chemical, and pharmaceutical industries [22–24]. On the other hand, Sacha inchi (Plukenetia volubilis L.) is known as the Inca peanut and is a perennial oilseed plant of the Euphorbiaceae family that is native to South America [25,26]. The oil extracted from oleaginous plants is a source of proteins and nutrients and has phytochemical properties [25,26].

Despite the popularity of wide-ranging natural oils, vegetable oils from avocado, buriti, Sacha inchi, and passion fruit have been explored with particular interest in the synthesis of carbon dots (C-dots) and their possible interdisciplinary applications. Sacha inchi, passion fruit, and avocado oils have been used for silver, gold, and copper oxide nanoparticle synthesis and stabilization [27–31]. However, some oils from vegetables to waste oils have been reported for use in C-dot synthesis [32–35]. C-dots with cytotoxic and apoptotic effects on cancer cell lines were synthesized from walnut oil via a hydrothermal method [32]. Several Indian essential herb oils derived from clove (Eugenia caryophyllata), basil (Ocimum basilicium), turmeric (Curcuma longa), and cardamom (Elettaria cardamomum) were used for C-dot synthesis and were tested for potential antifungal activity and antiproliferative nature [33]. On the other hand, waste engines and frying oils have been used to C-dots in a polymer matrix for Fe3+ detection [34] and as a carbon source for sulfur-doped carbon dots (S-Cdots) [35], respectively.

The present work reports the thermo-optical characterization of the natural vegetable oils of avocado (Persea gratissima), buriti (Mauritia flexuosa L. f.), passion fruit (Passiflora Edulis), and Sacha inchi (Plukenetia Volubilis). The refractive index temperature coefficient (dn/dT) was determined via simple Mach–Zehnder interferometry [36]. Thermal lens (TL) [37,38] and resolved fluorescence lifetime [38,39] techniques were applied to characterize the fluorescence quantum efficiency (
η
) and fluorescence lifetime (
τ
) of Voil, respectively. The avocado (Persea gratissima) oil was heat treated at 180 °C for C-dot synthesis via the hydrothermal method. The
η
and
τ
values obtained for avocado-based C-dots were compared with those from other carbon source oils reported as having potential for bioapplications [40,41].

The avocado (100 mL, Lote 274), buriti (100 mL, Lote 109), and passion fruit (500 mL, Lote 138) edible oils were obtained from Farquima Company in São Paulo State, Brazil, and Sacha inchi (250 mL) oil was purchased from the market naturist in Ayacucho, Perú.

Avocado oil-based carbon dots were synthesized via a hydrothermal method. An amount of 18 mL of avocado oil was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel laboratory autoclave, heated in an oven at 180 °C for 10 h, and then cooled to room temperature. The sample obtained was centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 15 min twice to remove large or agglomerated particles, and the supernatants containing C-dots were further purified via a 220 nm membrane.

Absorption spectra were obtained via a quartz cuvette with a Shimadzu UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV-2600i). Fluorescence spectra (excitation wavelength at 460 nm) were obtained via a portable spectrometer (Ocean Optics USB2000+). Fluorescence at various wavelengths (350–550 nm) was measured via a Shimadzu RF-5301 PC spectrofluorophotometer. Time-resolved fluorescence lifetime (TRFL) measurements were performed by using a light source at λe = (460 ± 10) nm and 40 μW average power at 40 MHz and 800 ps (PLS 450 LED). The TRFL results for the oil samples were analyzed with a FluoTime 100 time-resolved fluorescence spectrometer from PicoQuant using a 1 cm thick quartz cuvette [38]. A Ludox solution was used as a scattering sample for prompt measurement [38,39]. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was performed using a PerkinElmer Frontier spectrometer with a resolution of 2 cm−1. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) imaging of the C-dots was recorded at room temperature with a scanning probe microscope (SPM-9600, Shimadzu). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was carried out via a Hitachi (HT7700, 80 kV), and the frequency of the C-dot diameters was processed by ImageJ software.

The refractive index temperature coefficient (dn/dT) was determined via a Mach–Zehnder type interferometric setup with a single-arm dual interferometer at λ = 532 nm. For liquids, thermal expansion was not measured because it only increases the height inside the cuvette. Neglecting the expansion of the cuvette glass (
α = 0

where ΔT is the temperature spacing between two consecutive fringes and L is the thickness of the cuvette (L = 1 mm).

The TL effect is created when an excitation laser beam passes through a sample cuvette of thickness L, and the absorbed energy is converted into heat. Heat deposition, via nonradiative decay processes, changes the solution's refractive index and causes a thermally induced phase change [37,42,43]. The TL effect changes the divergence of a probe laser beam that passes through a sample. The time dependence of the variation in the intensity I(t) of the on-axis probe beam (with wavelength λ
p ) can be calculated in the central part of the probe laser beam in the continuous wave (CW) excitation regime as [42]:

In the dual-beam mode-mismatched TL configuration using pump and probe beams, the TL transient signal amplitude (θ) of the probe beam between r = 0 and r =

em
> is the average emission wavelength. In this work, transient TL measurements were performed in the mode-mismatched dual-beam (excitation and probe) configuration. A CW He–Ne laser (λp = 632.8 nm, and Pe ≈ 1 mW) was used as the probe beam, and a CW diode laser (λe = 532 nm) with excitation power between Pe = 1–20 mW was applied, modulated by a chopper, as the excitation beam. The parameters m = 7.866, V = 1.74, and
w e

μ m
were determined in this TL configuration.

Fig. 1 presents the dn/dT as a function of temperature (∼296-329 K−1) for different vegetable oils, such as avocado (a), buriti (b), passion fruit (c), and Sacha inchi (d) oils. The dn/dT values obtained are approximately constant in the measured temperature range. Table 1 presents the refractive index values (n) and dn/dT average values for vegetable oils performed at 532 nm in the evaluated temperature range. The refractive index n of each sample was measured, and the results obtained agreed with the manufacturer's report. Limited results are presented in the literature for the temperature dependence of the refractive index and the refractive index temperature coefficient for vegetable oils [45,46]. In addition, for olive oil, dn/dT values between 10−4–10−3 K-1 for 298–373 K are reported [46].

Fig. 2 presents the fluorescence and UV–vis absorbance spectra for the AO, BO, PFO, and SIO vegetable oils. The average fluorescence emission wavelength <λ
em
> values for all Voil are presented in Table 1. Compared with other natural oils, avocado oil presents a greater fluorescence area. The emission peak that appears for BO, PFO, and SIO at ∼670 nm can be attributed to chlorophyll fluorescence [16,47]. The typical electronic spectra for the four investigated oils were observed in the UV–vis spectral region, as reported for other oils, such as lavender in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and white sesame (WS) oil [47,48]. For WS oil, a bant at 287 nm was detected and assigned to conjugated trienes [47]. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the AO pulp extract is presented in Fig. 2, and the maximum absorption peak was observed at ∼269 nm, which was attributed to possible trienes formation and polyphenolic compounds [49–51]. For BO, the UV–vis absorption spectrum shows the maximum absorption peak at 272 nm, which is attributed to the electronic transitions (π – π∗) originating from the CC of the fatty acids and their oxidative derivatives [52], and the band between 400 and 500 nm is due to carotenoids [52,53]. In addition, for PFO and SIO, the maximum absorption peaks are observed at ∼270 nm and 280 nm, and these bands are attributed to the electronic transitions (π–π∗) and (n–π∗) due to CC and CO, respectively, of fatty acids and their oxidative derivatives [52,54].

The FTIR spectra for all the Voil samples are presented in Fig. 3 between 4000 and 500 cm−1 to enable identification of the investigated oils. Fats and oils are reported to be esters of glycerol with three fatty acids [55]. The functional groups observed in the absorption bands at 3007
c m
− 1
for AO, 3011
c m
− 1
for SIO, and 3005
c m
− 1
for BO and PFO are attributed to = C–H stretching of the olefinic cis-double bonds, and this absorption band is reported to be indicative of unsaturated fatty acids in Voil [55–57]. The absorption band at 2923
c m
− 1
for AO, BO, and PFO; and at 2925
c m
− 1
for SIO is due to methylene asymmetric stretching of –CH2 [55,56]. At 2853
c m
− 1
for BO and PFO, 2854
c m
− 1
for AO and SIO are attributed to the methylene symmetric stretching of –CH2, which all belong to the alkane and alkene groups [55,56], whereas a band at 1744
c m
− 1
for AO, BO and PFO, and a band at 1743
c m
− 1
for SIO are assigned to the CO stretching of the ester group [56,58], which is characteristic of oils with a high degree of unsaturation [56]. The small band at 1657
c m
− 1
for AO, 1654
c m
− 1
for BO, PFO and SIO corresponds to the cis–CC– stretching of the unsaturated acyl group and fatty acids in Voil [56,57,59]. The band at 1457
c m
− 1
for AO, BO, PFO and SIO is assigned to the bending vibrations of CH2 and CH3 aliphatic groups [56,60]. A typical band for Voil reported at 1160
c m
− 1
is attributed to the stretching vibrations of the C–O in the ester groups and the bending vibration of the CH2 group [56]. The band at 1094
c m
− 1
is due to –C–O stretching, and the 721
c m
− 1
band is associated with an isolated trans alkene group (–HCCH–) and –CH2 bending [55,56,61,62]. Table 2 presents the main FTIR absorption bands and functional groups for all investigated Voil [56,59,60,62,63].

Fig. 4 shows a TL transient signal for AO, and in this case, the TL effect created defocusing of the probe beam in the far field due to a negative value of dn/dT. The experimental data were fitted by Eq. (2), and the parameters θ and τ
c were determined. The thermal diffusivity D was obtained from τ
c via Eq. (3) for AO at room temperature, and the average D values are presented in Table 3 for all vegetable oils (AO, BO, PFO, and SIO). The thermal diffusivity obtained for Voil is in the range of (0.96–1.05) x 10−3 cm2/s (Table 3). Compared with those of other vegetable edible oils, such as corn, canola, and sunflower, the D values reported are (0.82–0.89) x 10−3 cm2/s, (0.89–0.96) x 10−3 cm2/s and (0.88–0.89) x 10−3 cm2/s, respectively [64,65].

The average thermal parameter for avocado oil, Θ = θ/PeαLef, was determined from the transient TL measurements using different powers of the excitation beam (Fig. 4). From Eq. (4), using the parameter dn/dT (Table 1), the value of K = 0.17 W m−1 K−1 for Voil [66,67], and an average value of φ = (0.32 ± 0.03) was determined. Considering λ
e = 532 nm and <λ
em
> presented in Table 1, the average value of the fluorescence quantum yield η = (0.71 ± 0.06) was obtained. Table 3 shows all the average results calculated for the Voil studied. To the best of the authors' knowledge, the η values reported for Voil oils in the literature are limited. A fluorescence quantum yield of η = 0.57 at 632 nm was reported for olive oil [68]. However, fluorescence quantum yield results are reported for Brunei crude oils between 0.7 and 0 using an excitation wavelength of ∼3Figure 6(b) presents the fluorescence spectra for50–900 nm [69]. In addition, relative fluorescence quantum yields are reported for different trademarks of crude oils that decrease as a function of the chromophore concentration [70].

Fig. 5 presents the time-resolved fluorescence (TRFL) spectroscopy results for all vegetable oils. TRFL measurements of the excited states performed for AO and BO were adjusted via a single exponential A + B∗Exp (-t/τ1), where A and B are constants, and where τ
1 is the fluorescence lifetime. The fluorescence decays were well fitted using a χ 2 value less than 1.004 for all Voil. The TRFL setup was tested in sodium fluorescein (Synth) in an alkaline aqueous solution (pH 11.24). The transient fluorescence for fluorescein was adjusted with a single exponential (τ = (4.13 ± 0.01) ns and χ 2 = (1.03 ± 0. 01)) [38,71]. For PFO and SIO, the TRFL measurements were adjusted via two exponentials B + A1
∙
exp (-t/τ1) + A2
∙
exp (-t/τ2), and the average fluorescence lifetime τ
AVE was determined via the following expression [72,73]:
(5)
τ AVE
=

)

where τ
AVE is the amplitude-weighted average lifetime of fluorescence and where τ
1 and τ
2 are the time constants of the fast and slow decay processes, respectively. A
1 and A
2 are the corresponding fractional values of the fluorescence intensities. A fluorescence lifetime τAVE of ∼3.2 ns was obtained for the AO, BO, PFO, and SIO oils. The average results of the TRFL and TL techniques were performed on three independent sets of fresh oil samples, and each set of measurements was repeated approximately seven times. In addition, TRFL measurements have been reported for vegetable oils such as camellia oil, peanut oil, and sunflower oil via three exponentials in transient fitting, where τ1 ≈ 1.3–2.3 ns and the components with longer lifetimes in pure oils are less significant [74]. For borage oil, lifetimes of τ1 ≈ 2.07 ns (A1 = 59.7 %) and τ2 ≈ 0.73 ns (A2 = 26.5 %) at 455 nm have been reported [75]. In summary, TRFL measurements at λ
e = 405 nm are reported for olive oil when τ1 ≈ 4.16 ns (A1 = 15.4 %) and τ2 ≈ 6.99 ns (A2 = 84.6 %) [76], for rapeseed oil when τ1 ≈ 0.12 ns (A1 = 92.5 %) and τ2 ≈ 6.32 ns (A2 = 7.5 %) [76], and for sunflower oil when τ1 ≈ 0.09 ns (A1 = 96.9 %) and τ2 ≈ 5.92 ns (A2 = 3.1 %) [76].

Compared with the BO, PFO, and SIO oils, the AO oil presented greater fluorescence intensity and η. The AO was subsequently subjected to heat treatment at 180 °C, for the synthesis of C-dots as an avocado-based carbon source. Fig. 6 presents the absorbance, fluorescence, and AFM results for AO after thermal treatment as a preliminary nanoparticle (CAO-dot) synthesized as a possible oil application. The absorbance spectrum for CAO-dots (Fig. 6 (a)) presents a band at ∼ 269 nm, which is typical for carbon dots obtained from different materials, attributed to the π–π∗ transition of the CC bond [77,78], and a shoulder at 315 nm, reported to be due to n–π∗ transitions of the CO and CN bonds [77,78]. Fig. 6(b) presents the fluorescence spectra for CAO-dots at different excitation wavelengths (λe = 350–550 nm). The value of <λ
em
> = (544 ± 1) nm was obtained at λe = 460 nm. The inset presents normalized fluorescence spectra (Fig. 6(c)), and the fluorescence shift is reported as evidence of nanoparticle formation [79,80]. This excitation dependence of the fluorescence spectra is attributed to defects in the energy levels produced by surface passivation, possible recombination radiation from excitons trapped by defects, and multiple transition modes [79,80]. Fig. 6(d and e) presents the AFM results for the CAO-dots and the height distributions measured [81]. The average height distribution of the CAO-dots obtained from the AFM measurements was ∼1.3 nm (Fig. 6(e)). Fig. 7 presents the TEM image, and the diameter frequency obtained for nanoparticle synthetized. The average size determined is between (8–10) nm for CAO-dots. TL measurements and TRFL were applied in CAO-dots for η = 0.31 and τ =3.42 ns determination, and the results are presented in Tables 3 and 4 , respectively. Furthermore, Table 5 presents the η and τ parameters for different C-dots synthesized from other oil carbon sources and potential applications for these eco-friendly nanomaterials [32,40,41,82–84]. The η and τ values obtained for the CAO-dots are similar to the results presented for other C-dots (Table 5), highlighting avocado oil as a carbon source as a potential material for possible bioapplication. Recent studies are underway using C-dots and/or vegetable oils incorporated into healing and antibacterial biofilms, for possible applications in dressings and functional packaging [85,86].

Thermo-optical characterization was performed for vegetable oils (Voil), such as avocado (AO), buriti (BO), passion fruit (PFO), and sacha inchi (SIO). The thermo-optical coefficients (dn/dT) were determined as approximately constant parameters in the range of (296–329) K−1 for all Voil. The measured thermal diffusivity (D) values are in the range of (0.96–1.05) x 10−3 cm2/s for all Voil. The fluorescence quantum yield η = (0.5–0.71) and the amplitude-weighted average lifetimes of fluorescence τAVE = (2.4–3.7) ns were determined for Voil. A relatively high η value was determined for avocado oil compared with BO, PFO, and SIO. Avocado oil was heated to 180 °C for the synthesis of carbon dots (CAO-dots) with an approximate height distribution of ∼1.3 nm, an average diameter obtained of 9.1 nm, η = 0.31 and τ = 3.42 ns, aiming as a material for potential bioapplications.

Teófanes B. Serna: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Dora G. Felipe: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Investigation. Acácio A. Andrade: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Viviane Pilla: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.
